<par>Sak also has a compound <latinform>u-kyi</latinform> 'egg', which apparently means "bird-shit", where we interpret the first syllable as meaning 'bird', not 'egg'. Cf. the Jingpho prefix <latinform>ù-</latinform>, which occurs in many bird-related words, e.g. <latinform>ù-dì</latinform> 'egg', <latinform>ù-mài</latinform> 'bird's tail' (more examples in Hanson, pp. 50-53). <xref ref="1603">#1603</xref> seems related to <reconstruction>*wu</reconstruction> EGG/BIRD in any case.</par>

<par>Also means "depression between horns of cattle”; the second syllable might be <latinform>wám</latinform> ‘shake, as a tree; move up and down’  (H. 705) (cf. <latinform>r̀dblquote awám</latinform> ‘move slowly up and down (H. 35), <latinform>kr̀dblquote awám</latinform> ‘wave, as the waves, long grass, flowing garments, etc.) Maran adds <latinform>r̀dblquote awàm</latinform> ‘undulate, sway when moved’  and give the tones <latinform>bùŋ wàm</latinform>. The semantic association is via the often visible pulse in a fontanel.</par>

<par>The <emph>Jinghpo-Chinese Dictionary</emph> (Dai, et al. 1983:566) cites the form <latinform>ne³¹</latinform> '<hanform>性交</hanform> [have sexual congress]'  with no final glottal stop, making it look identical to the word for 'penis'.</par>

<par>Jäschke (p. 61) notes that this word is "the usual, not exactly obscene, yet not euphemistic term for it". It also has the non-sexual meanings 'cohere, stick together' and 'be cloudy, overcast (of the sky)'.</par>

<par>The basic meaning of Karbi <latinform>rap</latinform> is 'to befriend; to be together'. It occurs as an auxiliary after other verbs (e.g. <latinform>do</latinform> 'be; exist', <latinform>i</latinform> 'sleep'), meaning 'to V together'.</par>
<par>This is a euphemism, similar to English <emph>get together</emph>.</par>
<par>Is this an O-V construction? Perhaps <latinform>che</latinform> is reflexive.</par>

<par>Although Lp <latinform>myal</latinform> could come from <reconstruction>*s-mul</reconstruction>, with <reconstruction>*s- </reconstruction>regularly > Lp. <latinform>-y-</latinform>, there's no explanation for the <latinform>a</latinform> ± <latinform>i</latinform> ± <latinform>u</latinform> alternation. Better set up <latinform>-u-</latinform> ± [<latinform>-i-</latinform> ± <latinform>-ya-</latinform>] (cf. EYE), so that the <latinform>-y-</latinform> has extra-Lepcha etymological status.</par>

<par>This Akha reflex is to be referred to the present etymon, rather than to <xref ref="1284">#1284</xref>, since Akha <latinform>-oe</latinform> is the regular reflex of PTB <reconstruction>*-əw</reconstruction> (e.g. STEAL <reconstruction>*r-kəw</reconstruction> > Akha <latinform>k'oe˯</latinform> ; WEEP <reconstruction>*ŋəw</reconstruction> > Ak. <latinform>ngoeˇ</latinform>). For the Akha reflex of PLB <reconstruction>*r-lik</reconstruction> 'testicle', see <latinform>leh˰</latinform>, above <xref ref="1286">#1286</xref>. See also the note on Akha <latinform>beuˆ leuˆ</latinform> 'penis (polite)', above <xref ref="1284">#1284</xref>.</par>
<par>WT sgro-ba also means 'bark of a species of willow'. But sgro also means 'large feather, esp. quill-feather, used for an ornament of arrows, as a charm. etc.' Jäschke 122.</par>

<par>The first syllable of this form resembles the second syllable of Kham <latinform>or-'kal</latinform> 'penis'. However, according to Mainwaring/Grünwedel 1898:53, the morpheme <latinform>gar</latinform> actually means 'curved, crooked at one end, bent'.</par>
<par>The second syllable is homophonous with Tangkhul <latinform>kui</latinform> 'head', though this seems to be fortuitous, since the form does not mean 'glans'.</par>
<par>Tangkhul <latinform>kui</latinform> is homophonous with <latinform>kui</latinform> 'head', though this seems to be fortuitous, since <latinform>shaŋ kui</latinform> does not mean 'glans'.</par>

<par>Lepcha <latinform>t'un</latinform> means 'skin; hide' in isolation (GBM-Lepcha p. 154), though the prefixed form <latinform>a-t'un</latinform> is glossed either 'skin' (pp. 154, 532) or 'foreskin' (p. 491).</par>
<par>WB <latinform>pûn</latinform> 'hide (v.)'; <latinform>-liŋ-</latinform> is a borrowing from Sanskrit <latinform>lingam</latinform> 'private parts, penis'.</par>
<par>This form goes back to PLB Tone *1. It is the allofam <latinform>sûi</latinform> (< PLB Tone *2) which is directly cognate to Lahu <latinform>šɔ̄</latinform>.</par>
<par>Lepcha <latinform>pă-tin̊</latinform>, seems to mean 'small stick, switch' (Mainwaring, pp. 127-8).</par>

<par>This form reflects an alternate prototype with sibilant prefix, <reconstruction>*s-ley</reconstruction> (cf. Bisu <latinform>hlè</latinform> 'penis', where the voiceless lateral also points to <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> instead of <reconstruction>*m-</reconstruction>).</par>
<par>Cf. Limbu. 2d syll of CAUL Tangkhul? Any membraneous matter covering something, as a spider’ s web.</par>

<par>This curious form receives four glosses in Jäschke, p. 231: (1) 'spot (as on a leopard)'; (2) 'zero, naught'; (3) <emph>‘semen virile’</emph>; (4) 'contemplation'. Jäschke remarks that the last two senses are "mystically connected with each other" in Buddhist thought. (The sense of 'naught' seems like a recent extension of 'spot', since a small zero looks like a dot.) On <emph>a priori</emph> semantic grounds, another WT word, <latinform>t'igs-pa</latinform> 'drop (of liquid)' also looks like it might be related; but in other Himalayish languages similar forms have no liquid associations, e.g. Bunan <latinform>tig-pa</latinform> 'testicle' and Lepcha <latinform>t'ik</latinform> 'penis'. It looks as if 'spot/zero' and 'semen/contemplation' are mere homophones.</par>

<par>The <latinform>-d-</latinform> in Bodo <latinform>da</latinform> is a plausible intervocalic reflex of <reconstruction>*-r-</reconstruction>. The name of this language itself is often transcribed as "Boro". See Bhat 1968.</par>
<par>Tangkhul <latinform>hai ra</latinform> certainly looks like a case of "genital flipflop", i.e. semen is viewed as "vagina-semen", since that is its destination.</par>

<par>Lahu <latinform>phɨ</latinform> occurs in many other Lahu compounds, including <latinform>á-thɔ-phɨ</latinform> 'scabbard; sheath' ("knife-nest"), <latinform>ú-gɛ̂-phɨ</latinform> 'pillow-case', <latinform>khá-cè-phɨ</latinform> 'quiver for arrows', <latinform>yɨ̀ʔ-phɨ</latinform> 'bed' ("sleep-nest"), <latinform>lìʔ-phɨ</latinform> 'envelope', etc.</par>

<par>The last element means 'nest; receptacle'. See <xref ref="1611">#1611</xref>, Chapter VI. It occurs in many other Lahu compounds, including <latinform>yâ-phɨ</latinform> 'placenta' ("child-nest"), <latinform>á-thɔ-phɨ</latinform> 'scabbard; sheath' ("knife-nest"), <latinform>ú-gɛ̂-phɨ</latinform> 'pillow-case', <latinform>khá-cè-phɨ</latinform> 'quiver for arrows', <latinform>yɨ̀ʔ-phɨ</latinform> 'bed' ("sleep-nest"), <latinform>lìʔ-phɨ</latinform> 'envelope', etc. </par>
<par>The last two syllables are probably a borrowing from Nepali <latinform>pèmdash ato</latinform> ‘shoulder’ , but if not they could be tagged 570 *ba SHOULDER + 3349 *ta SHOULDER.</par>

<par><latinform>cho</latinform> related to <latinform>khaw</latinform> next or <latinform>cham</latinform> below?? M2: 654?</par>
<par>The last syllable means 'hole' (see <xref ref="3432">#3432</xref>, below). See G. van Driem, <emph>A Grammar of Limbu</emph> (1987), p. 426.</par>
<par>Notice the reduction of the vowel to zero in Kanauri <latinform>ts</latinform>.</par>
<par>Note the variation between final velar nasal and zero-final in the Hayu forms. See also the similar Hayu reflexes of <xref ref="686">#686</xref>.</par>
<par>These Jingpho forms seem to reflect an open-syllable allofam <reconstruction>*ku</reconstruction>.</par>
<par>The first syllable <latinform>hi-</latinform> is a lookalike of the vulgar Siamese word <latinform>hǐi</latinform>.</par>

<par>In Mainwaring's transcription, <latinform>j</latinform> stands for [ʑ], or as he puts it, "like French j-" (p. 93). Curiously, although both <latinform>jak</latinform> and <latinform>yak</latinform> appear in the index (p. 540), neither appears in the body of the dictionary.</par>
<par>Gurung <latinform>prxe-ba</latinform> is cited in Noonan's (1999) <emph>Chantyal Dictionary and Texts</emph> under 'have sex'.</par>
<par>See also WT <latinform>gsan̊-ćʻab</latinform> 'urine (resp.)', lit. "secret-water". <latinform>ćʻab</latinform> is the respectful form for <latinform>c̀ʻu</latinform> 'water'.</par>
